title: What to know before using Eclipta prostrata (Trailing Eclipta)?
intro: Eclipta prostrata, also known as false daisy, is a widely used medicinal plant that has been traditionally used in various systems of medicine including Ayurveda and Chinese medicine. It is an annual herb with a prostrate growth habit, and it has opposite, sessile, oblong-spathulate leaves that are green in color and have a rough texture. This plant prefers moist, well-drained soil and can be found growing in disturbed areas, roadsides, and fields. The medicinal properties of Eclipta prostrata include hepatoprotective, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant activities. It is also used to treat a variety of ailments such as liver disorders, skin diseases, and hair loss. The plant contains several bioactive compounds such as wedelolactone, demethylwedelolactone, and polyphenols which are responsible for its medicinal properties.
medicine_desc: Eclipta prostrata, also known as false daisy, is a plant that has been used in traditional medicine for centuries. It is native to India and other tropical regions of the world and is often used in Ayurvedic medicine. The plant has a variety of medicinal uses, including as an anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and anti-cancer agent. It is also used to treat skin diseases, liver disorders, and respiratory conditions.

Eclipta prostrata contains a number of beneficial compounds that are responsible for its medicinal properties. These include flavonoids, triterpenes, and steroids. The flavonoids in Eclipta prostrata have been shown to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects. Triterpenes, such as eclipta-sterol and its derivatives, have been found to have hepatoprotective, antitumor, and immunomodulatory activities. Steroids, such as β-sitosterol and stigmasterol, have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antitumor effects.

There are several ways to prepare Eclipta prostrata for medicinal use. The leaves and stems of the plant can be made into a paste and applied topically to the skin to treat wounds, ulcers, and eczema. The plant can also be made into a tea or decoction and taken internally to treat liver disorders, respiratory conditions, and other ailments. Additionally, the plant can be dried and powdered and taken in capsule form.

Despite its many benefits, Eclipta prostrata can have some side effects. The plant can cause skin irritation and stomach upset in some people. It can also lower blood sugar levels, so people with diabetes should use it with caution. Additionally, it can interact with certain medications, such as blood thinners and sedatives, so it is important to speak with a healthcare provider before using Eclipta prostrata if you are taking any medications.

When using Eclipta prostrata, it is important to take certain precautions. It is recommended to use the plant under the guidance of a qualified healthcare practitioner. Pregnant and breastfeeding women should avoid using Eclipta prostrata, as its safety during pregnancy and lactation has not been established. Additionally, it is important to use the plant in moderation, as excessive use can cause liver toxicity. It is also important to make sure that the plant is properly identified and that it is free from contaminants before using it.
horticulture_desc: Eclipta prostrata, also known as false daisy, is a annual plant that is native to warm temperate and tropical regions of the world. This plant is a hardy one, able to grow in a variety of conditions, but there are certain growth requirements that should be met in order to ensure the best possible results. Eclipta prostrata prefers full sun, but it can also grow in partial shade. It can tolerate a wide range of soils, but prefers well-drained loamy soil. This plant is drought tolerant, but will benefit from regular watering, especially during dry spells.

When it comes to planting Eclipta prostrata, it is important to start with healthy seedlings or seeds. The seeds can be sown directly in the ground, or they can be started indoors and then transplanted outside. When planting, it is important to give the plants enough space to grow, as they can become quite large. A spacing of 12-18 inches between plants is usually sufficient. It is also important to keep the soil consistently moist during the first few weeks after planting, to help the plants establish themselves.

Caring for Eclipta prostrata is relatively easy. Once established, these plants are quite drought tolerant and do not require a lot of maintenance. However, they do benefit from regular watering, especially during dry spells. They also benefit from regular fertilization, especially if the soil is not particularly fertile. A balanced fertilizer, applied once or twice a year, is usually sufficient. It is also important to keep an eye out for weeds, as they can compete with the plants for resources.

Harvesting Eclipta prostrata is relatively simple. The leaves and stems can be harvested at any time, but it is best to wait until the plants are at least 6-8 inches tall. The leaves and stems can be harvested by cutting them off at the base of the plant. It is important to leave some of the leaves and stems on the plant, as this will help it to continue growing. The leaves and stems can be used fresh or they can be dried for later use.

Like any plant, Eclipta prostrata is susceptible to pests and diseases. Some of the most common pests include aphids, mealybugs, and spider mites. These pests can be controlled with insecticidal soap or horticultural oil. Diseases such as powdery mildew and leaf spot can also affect Eclipta prostrata. These can be controlled with fungicides. It is important to keep an eye out for these issues and to take action as soon as they are noticed, as they can quickly spread and cause significant damage.
botany_desc: Eclipta prostrata, also known as false daisy, is a species of flowering plant in the family Asteraceae. It is an annual or short-lived perennial herb that is native to tropical and subtropical regions of the world, including the Americas, Africa, and southern Asia. This species is a member of the Eclipta genus, which contains only a few other species. E. prostrata is the most widely distributed and well-studied member of the genus.

Eclipta prostrata is a small, low-growing plant that typically reaches a height of 30-60 cm. It has a prostrate or decumbent growth habit, with stems that root at the nodes. The leaves are opposite, simple, and entire, with a lanceolate or ovate shape and a smooth or slightly toothed margin. The leaves are arranged in a spiral pattern along the stem and are attached by short petioles. The plant produces small, white or lavender flowers in clusters at the ends of the stems. The flowers are radially symmetrical and have a discoid shape, with a central disc of florets surrounded by a ring of ray florets.

Eclipta prostrata is known by several other common names, including yerba de tago, mare's tail, and red saddlemeat. These names can be confusing, as they are also used to refer to other plants. For example, "mare's tail" is also used to describe Equisetum arvense, a completely different species in the Equisetopsida division. Despite the potential for confusion, E. prostrata is the only species in the Asteraceae family that is commonly called "mare's tail."

Eclipta prostrata is found in a wide range of habitats, including fields, meadows, pastures, and along the edges of roads and streams. It prefers moist, well-drained soils and can tolerate partial shade. It is often found in disturbed areas, such as construction sites and abandoned lots. It is a hardy plant that can tolerate a wide range of temperatures and growing conditions.

Eclipta prostrata has a relatively short life-cycle, typically completing its growth and reproduction within a few months. It reproduces by seed, which are produced in small, dry fruits called cypselae. The seeds are dispersed by wind and water, and can also be spread by animals. The seeds can remain viable in the soil for several years, allowing the plant to establish new populations in new locations. The plant also spreads through vegetative reproduction, with new plants growing from stem fragments that root at the nodes.
